name: Sabinares del Arlanza and Yecla Gorge Loop
name_original: Desfiladero de la Yecla y Alto del Horno — circular por el Parque natural Sabinares del Arlanza - La Yecla
description: This 44.3-mile route is listed for racebikes, but the Desfiladero de la Yecla gorge is a pedestrian-only path.
region_intro: Road cycling around Valdeande features routes through varied terrain, including gorges, hills, and forested areas. The region is characterized by its natural landscapes, such as the Sabinares del Arlanza and the Yecla Gorge. Riders can expect a mix of elevations, from gentle inclines to more challenging climbs. This area provides options for different road cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Valdeande The most popular road cycling route is Sabinares del Arlanza and Yecla Gorge Loop, a 44.3 miles (71.3 km) trail that takes 4 hours 1 minute to complete. This route explores a significant natural area with varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Yecla Gorge – Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) loop from Espinosa de Cervera, a moderate 41.7 miles (67.1 km) path. This route offers views of the gorge and includes a climb to Alto de Peña Alta. Local road cyclists also love the View of Peña de Carazo – Caleruega loop from Baños de Valdearados, a 56.5 miles (91.0 km) trail leading through the area around Peña de Carazo, often completed in about 4 hours 4 minutes. Road cycling around Valdeande is defined by gorges, hills, and forested landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy road bike routes Valdeande to more advanced road bike trails near Valdeande. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdeande's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Valdeande?
A: There are &lt;em&gt;over 5&lt;/em&gt; distinct road cycling routes around Valdeande documented on komoot, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Valdeande offers routes suitable for beginners. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38159787" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arauzo de Torre – Huerta del Rey loop from Arauzo de Torre&lt;/a&gt; is an easy route, covering about 26.6 km with minimal elevation gain, making it a good option for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Valdeande?
A: Road cycling around Valdeande features varied terrain, including gorges, rolling hills, and forested areas. You'll encounter a mix of elevations, from gentle inclines to more challenging climbs, especially around natural landscapes like the Sabinares del Arlanza and the Yecla Gorge.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. Experienced riders can tackle routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20149890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña de Carazo – Caleruega loop from Baños de Valdearados&lt;/a&gt;. This route is approximately 91 km long and features significant elevation changes, offering a demanding ride through the area around Peña de Carazo.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views. You can find natural beauty spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5728210" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balsa de Arauzo de Salce&lt;/a&gt; or historical sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5728195" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Cristóbal Hermitage and Watchtower&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5283553" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Cabeza Summit&lt;/a&gt;, which offers panoramic vistas.

Q: Are the road cycling routes around Valdeande typically loop routes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the Valdeande area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1055811840/sabinares-del-arlanza-and-yecla-gorge-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sabinares del Arlanza and Yecla Gorge Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31470077" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Yecla Gorge – Alto de Peña Alta (1078 m) loop from Espinosa de Cervera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical distance for road cycling routes in this region?
A: Route distances vary significantly to accommodate different preferences. You can find shorter routes around 26 km, such as the Arauzo de Torre loop, up to longer, more challenging rides like the View of Peña de Carazo – Caleruega loop, which extends to about 91 km.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Valdeande?
A: The road cycling routes around Valdeande are well-regarded by the komoot community. Over 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the varied terrain and the natural beauty of the landscapes, including the gorges and forested sections.

Q: Are there any lesser-known or 'hidden gem' routes for road cycling near Valdeande?
A: While specific 'hidden gems' are subjective, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31814660" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Peña de Carazo – Mamolar Village loop from Arauzo de Torre&lt;/a&gt; offer a slightly less frequented option. It's a moderate 57.3 km route that provides beautiful views and a good challenge away from the most popular trails.

Q: What natural areas or parks do the road cycling routes pass through?
A: Many routes traverse or offer views of significant natural areas. The region is known for the Sabinares del Arlanza and the dramatic Yecla Gorge, both of which are prominent features on several road cycling tours, providing stunning natural backdrops for your ride.

Q: Is Valdeande suitable for road cycling year-round, or is there a best season?
A: While specific weather data isn't provided, the varied terrain suggests that spring and autumn would likely offer the most comfortable temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the potential extremes of summer heat or winter cold. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.
tips: After the town of Araúzo de Miel appears the mountain pass called Alto del Horno. Beautiful mountain pass between pine forests.

The Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos is considered to be one of the most culturally and historically significant monasteries in Spain. If you are lucky, during a visit to the monastery you will be able to enjoy the world-famous Gregorian chants of the monks, who still cultivate and practice them today as ever. An album released by the monks was in the US charts for 53 weeks and went platinum three times.

The La Yecla gorge is a deep and narrow gorge excavated in the limestone banks so characteristic of the relief of the Peñas de Cervera, enabled to make a route through a series of footbridges. It is an easy and very attractive walk of a kilometer and a half.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for road cycling, especially through the Desfiladero de la Yecla?
faqs.answer: No, the Desfiladero de la Yecla itself is a narrow gorge designed exclusively for pedestrians, featuring footbridges and pathways. It is not possible to cycle through the gorge. While the komoot tour is categorized as 'racebike', this refers to a circular road cycling route that passes *by* the gorge and through the wider Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park, not directly through the pedestrian section of the gorge.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the road cycling route around the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park?
faqs.answer: The road cycling route primarily follows regional roads through the diverse landscapes of the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park. You can expect varied terrain, including sections through extensive juniper woodlands, oak and pine forests, and potentially some riverine areas. The route includes climbs like the Alto del Horno, contributing to the moderate difficulty.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The highlight of the route is the dramatic Viewpoint over the Yecla Gorge, where you can observe the narrow limestone cliffs and often spot griffon vultures. The surrounding Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park is renowned for its ancient juniper forests, some trees over 2,000 years old, and diverse wildlife. The Alto del Horno also offers scenic views as a mountain pass.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Desfiladero de la Yecla or start the cycling route?
faqs.answer: Parking is generally available near the entrance to the Desfiladero de la Yecla for those wishing to explore the gorge on foot. For the circular road cycling route, you can typically find parking in nearby towns like Santo Domingo de Silos, which serves as a good starting point and offers amenities.

faqs.question: Is the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park dog-friendly for cyclists or hikers?
faqs.answer: While the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park generally welcomes visitors with dogs, specific regulations may apply, especially regarding leash requirements and access to certain protected areas or natural monuments like the Desfiladero de la Yecla. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all visitors.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit for road cycling or exploring the gorge?
faqs.answer: The best time to visit for road cycling is typically spring or autumn when temperatures are milder and the scenery is vibrant. For exploring the Desfiladero de la Yecla gorge on foot, spring, summer, and autumn are ideal. Winter can be challenging due to potential ice on the walkways and stairs within the gorge, requiring appropriate gear.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park or the Desfiladero de la Yecla?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required to access the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park or the Desfiladero de la Yecla. However, it's always advisable to check local regulations before your visit, especially for any specific activities or guided tours.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other trails. Notable sections include parts of El Destierro, GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT, Camino de Santiago de la Lana.BTT, and Camino de Santiago de la Lana.Senderista. You may also encounter shorter segments of the Camino del Cid (Senderista) and Sabinar del Arlanza.

faqs.question: What cultural or historical sites can I see near the route?
faqs.answer: A significant cultural highlight near the route is the Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos, a historic religious building renowned for its Romanesque cloister and Gregorian chants. The town of Santo Domingo de Silos itself offers a charming historical atmosphere.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a road cycling trip on this route?
faqs.answer: For a road cycling trip, ensure you have a well-maintained racebike, appropriate cycling attire, a helmet, water bottles, and snacks. Given the moderate elevation gain, consider your fitness level. Always carry a basic repair kit, a phone, and navigation tools. Sunscreen and layers are recommended depending on the season.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the Desfiladero de la Yecla or Santo Domingo de Silos?
faqs.answer: Public transport options to reach the Desfiladero de la Yecla directly might be limited. However, Santo Domingo de Silos, a nearby town, is generally more accessible by bus from larger cities in the region. From Santo Domingo de Silos, you would need to arrange further transport or cycle to the gorge or the starting point of your route.

waypoints.name: Monasterio de Santo Domingo de Silos
